text: In this video, we would like to present an intra-anal fistulotomy with marsupialization.

We would like to start by reminding you of Park's classification of most common fistulas. And also the Goodsall's rule that states if the external opening is anterior to the line, the fistula usually runs directly into the anal canal.
And if the external opening is posterior to the line, the fistula usually curves to the posterior midline of the canal.
A fifty-year-old gentleman presented at our clinic complaining of constant secretion through anus. From his previous medical history he had intersphincteric fistula that was treated several times.
MRI was performed. High intersphincteric fistula was detected.

The video shows the internal opening of the fistula at the level of the dentate line in the posterior midline, with no visible external opening. A schematic representation of the fistula tract is displayed.
According to Park's classification, the fistula in our case was intersphincteric fistula with rectal opening and without a perineal opening.
And here the scheme of operation.
The patient is positioned in the prone jackknife position with the buttocks taped aside. Surgical retractors are being placed to expose the anal region for the procedure.
The surgeon probes the blind high intersphincteric fistula tract through the internal opening, observing several milliliters of purulent discharge. A swab is taken for microbiological analysis.
The surgeon performs a phthalotomy using monopolar electrocautery, with a probe placed from the internal opening to the end of the blind intersphincteric fistula tract.
The surgeon performs curettage on the open fistula tract, removing debris and tissue from the exposed intersphincteric fistula.
The surgeon performs marsupialization on the fistula tract, creating an open pouch by suturing the edges of the tract to the surrounding skin.
The surgeon inserts a hemostatic sponge into the surgical site, likely to control bleeding within the exposed anal region.

Here you can see patient two months after operation. As the patient did not complain of fecal incontinence either before or after operation, the anorectal manometry was not indicated and not performed.
Wexner's score before and after operation was zero.
